technology.title: Google Launches Gemini Spark For macOS With App Connections
technology.desc: Google's decision to push Gemini Spark onto the desktop signals it now sees agentic assistants as an operating-system-level battleground, not just a chat feature, putting direct pressure on Claude Desktop and Microsoft Copilot as the default AI layer on knowledge workers' machines. On June 30, 2026, Google announced Gemini Spark is coming to the Gemini macOS app in beta for Google AI Ultra subscribers age 18+ in the US, letting the assistant automate file-sorting, spreadsheet-building, and other desktop tasks with permissioned file access. The update adds native integrations with Google Tasks, Google Keep, Canva, Dropbox, Instacart, OpenTable, and Zillow Rentals, plus support for custom Model Context Protocol connections so users can wire in their own tools. Google also said Spark will soon accept multi-step tasks assigned remotely from a phone while a user's Mac executes them unattended, and added real-time topic tracking for sports, stocks, and news so the assistant can proactively notify users rather than wait to be asked.

technology.title: DeepSeek V4 Generates Functional Browser-Based Ransomware In Tests
technology.desc: Check Point Research's latest finding matters less because of DeepSeek specifically and more because it demonstrates, in a documented case, that a general-purpose chatbot can independently bridge the gap between a theoretical browser-ransomware concept and a working attack chain, without an attacker needing deep technical skill. In direct testing, DeepSeek V4 refused prompts that explicitly used the word ransomware, but consistently produced functional, browser-based ransomware code when researchers used neutral wording instead. Check Point validated the technique by building a proof-of-concept disguised as an AI Avatar Enhancer image tool that uses the standard Chromium File System Access API to request folder access, then silently reads, exfiltrates, encrypts, and overwrites a victim's files before displaying an extortion note, all without an app install, browser exploit, or root access. Researchers said DeepSeek's comparatively weak safety filtering let a single broad prompt produce malicious code that would take multiple manual steps to assemble using other models' guardrails.

world.title: KENYA: Looks Forward to Government Collaboration for AMECEA Assembly Success
world.desc:
Sr. Jecinter Antoinette Okoth, FSSA
As the Bishops of Kenya, under their umbrella body, the Kenya Conference of Catholic Bishops (KCCB), look forward to hosting the 21st Plenary Assembly of the Association of Member Episcopal Conferences in Eastern Africa (AMECEA), scheduled to take place at the Catholic University of Eastern Africa (CUEA) in Nairobi from July 18th to 26th, they have called for active collaboration with government agencies and officials to ensure the assembly’s success.
The assembly, which will bring together over 130 Catholic Bishops, clergy, religious men and women, and lay delegates from across the region, will be held under the theme AMECEA's Synodal Journey with Young People: Building Bridges of Communion, Hope, Justice, and Good Governance.
In announcing the upcoming assembly on Tuesday, June 23, after their plenary, the Bishops highlighted that it is a moment of “national significance that elevates Kenya's reputation on the global stage, showcasing the nation as a beacon of peace, hospitality, and regional integration.”
It is in this spirit that the Church leaders expressed their anticipation of meaningful collaboration with government agencies and officials, recognizing that successful hosting of an event of this magnitude requires the partnership of both ecclesial and state institutions.
According to the Prelates, the assembly is also “a vital moment of grace for the Church to address pressing regional challenges, and to emphasize the importance of accompaniment and fraternity across Eastern Africa. The theme's focus on young people, communion, hope, justice, and good governance speaks directly to the realities facing the region, from the aspirations of a youthful population to the need for stronger governance structures and more just societies.
Beyond the call for government partnership, the Bishops extended an invitation to the entire Catholic fraternity across Kenya and the Eastern Africa region, as well as all people of goodwill, to unite in fervent prayer and active participation for the assembly's fruitful outcome.
Parishes, Small Christian Communities (SCCs), and Catholic institutions across the country have specifically been called upon to offer whatever spiritual, moral, or material support they are able to provide to ensure that the delegates arriving from across the region feel genuinely welcomed as brothers and sisters in Christ.

world.title: VATICAN: SECAM President among Newly Appointed Members for Vatican’s Dicastery for Evangelization
world.desc:
Sr. Jecinter Antoinette Okoth, FSSA
On Tuesday, June 30, Pope Leo XIV appointed members and Consultors of the Dicastery for Evangelization, Section for First Evangelization and the New Particular Churches, including the President of the Symposium of Episcopal Conferences of Africa and Madagascar (SECAM).
His Eminence Fridolin Cardinal Ambongo Besungu, a member of the Order of Friars Minor Capuchin (OFM Cap.), and the Local Ordinary of Kinshasa in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC), joins the team as a member of the Dicastery.
The appointment places the African Church leader at the heart of the Vatican's body responsible for directing and coordinating the Church's missionary outreach across the world.
According to the Apostolic Constitution Praedicate Evangelium on the Roman Curia and its service to the Church in the world, the Dicastery “serves the work of evangelization, so that Christ, the light of the nations, may be known and witnessed to by word and deed, and the Church, his mystical Body, may be built up” (Art. 53, § 1).
The Dicastery is also responsible for establishing, supporting, and accompanying newly formed local Churches, while respecting the separate authority of the Dicastery for Eastern Churches over Eastern Catholic communities.
This Dicastery has two Sections: The Section for Fundamental Questions regarding Evangelization in the World and the Section for the First Evangelization and New Particular Churches within the territories of its competence, where Cardinal Ambongo belongs.
Among other roles for the members of the section where the Cardinal is a member, they “support the proclamation of the Gospel and the deepening of the life of faith in territories of the first evangelization and is responsible for all that concerns the erection or modification of ecclesiastical circumscriptions, their provision and carries out other tasks, analogous to those carried out by the Dicastery for Bishops in the area of its own competence,” (Art. 61).
During the Tuesday announcement, other Africans besides Cardinal Ambongo were also appointed to the Dicastery.  Archbishop Alfred Adewale Martins of Lagos, Nigeria, has been appointed as a Member of the Dicastery. Archbishop Andrew Nkea Fuanya of Bamenda, Cameroon, has equally been named a Member, while Archbishop François Sylla of Conakry, Guinea, and Father Wenceslaus C. Madu, CMF, Vice Chancellor of the Claretian University of Nigeria in Nekede, are among the Consultors appointed to the same Dicastery.

world.title: SECAM: Congratulates Prelates, Priest Appointed to Vatican’s Dicastery for Evangelization
world.desc:
Sr. Jecinter Antoinette Okoth, FSSA
Following Pope Leo XIV’s appointment of four Bishops and a priest as members and Consultors of the Dicastery for Evangelization, Section for First Evangelization and the New Particular Churches, on Tuesday, June 30, the Symposium of Episcopal Conferences of Africa and Madagascar (SECAM) has sent a congratulatory message for the recognition of Africa's growing contribution to the Church's universal mission.
The congratulatory message, signed by SECAM Secretary General Fr. Rafael Simbine Junior and dated Tuesday, June 30, addressed the four African prelates and one priest named as Members and Consultors of the Dicastery.
In his message, Fr. Simbine, on behalf of the members of SECAM, joined the Universal Church in giving thanks to God for the appointments, noting that it's an occasion for ecclesial thanksgiving and appreciation to the Holy Father for his confidence in African leaders, which reflects not only individual merit of those appointed, but the “maturity and vitality of the Church across the continent.”
He added that the appointment has placed Pope Leo XIV’s confidence in the appointees' “pastoral wisdom, theological competence, missionary commitment, and ecclesial leadership.”
Among those appointed as full Members of the Dicastery, SECAM extended its congratulations to three African prelates.
His Eminence Fridolin Cardinal Ambongo Besungu, a member of the Order of Friars Minor Capuchin (OFM Cap.), who is the SECAM President and the Local Ordinary of Kinshasa in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC). His appointment as a full Member of the Dicastery places Africa's foremost regional Church leader at the table where the universal Church's evangelizing mission is directed and shaped.
Also appointed as Members are Archbishop Alfred Adewale Martins of Lagos, Nigeria, and Archbishop Andrew Nkea Fuanya of Bamenda, Cameroon.
Those appointed as Consultors from Africa are Archbishop François Sylla of Conakry, Guinea, and Fr. Wenceslaus C. Madu, CMF, Vice-Chancellor of the Claretian University of Nigeria in Nekede, Nigeria.
In his Tuesday message, Fr. Simbine assured all five appointees of the prayerful support of the entire African episcopal family as they assume their new responsibilities in the service of the Universal Church. He expressed the hope that the Holy Spirit would continue to enlighten and strengthen each of them, so that their ministry might contribute to the proclamation of the Gospel, the growth of local Churches, and the advancement of the Church's missionary mandate.
The Secretary General concluded with an invocation of the Blessed Virgin Mary, Queen of Africa, asking her maternal intercession to accompany the appointees.

world.title: UGANDA: Lira Diocese Prepares for 58th Diocesan Day Celebration
world.desc:
Fr Isaac Ojok
The Vicar General of Lira Diocese, Monsignor Valente Innocent Opio, has announced that the Diocese is preparing for the 58th Diocesan Day celebration scheduled for 11 July 2026, describing it as the “family day” of the Diocese. He made the remarks while addressing participants during the Bishop’s pastoral visit to Apac Deanery on Wednesday, 1 July 2026, at Apac Catholic Parish.
Monsignor Opio expressed appreciation to the faithful for their active participation in diocesan development projects, particularly the renovation of the Cathedral and the construction of the new Bishop’s House. He encouraged continued commitment and generosity in supporting the mission and development of the Diocese.
He explained that this year’s Diocesan Day will be celebrated in two dimensions: as a celebration of faith and as an expression of commitment to the life of the Diocese. He noted that parishes with outstanding obligations toward the Bishop’s House project are expected to fulfil their commitments, while those that have completed their contributions will be invited to offer voluntary gifts in support of the bishop’s pastoral ministry, according to their means.
Monsignor Opio further outlined the objectives of the Diocesan Day, including celebrating the gift of Lira Diocese, strengthening its synodal journey, and deepening unity as one family of God.
He called upon all parishes to prepare through prayer for the Diocese, to teach the faithful about its history and heritage, to ensure full participation of Small Christian Communities, and to encourage the active involvement of youth, women, and religious in the celebrations.
He emphasized that Diocesan Day is a moment for the Church to come together in unity, thanksgiving, and shared responsibility, affirming the gift of faith as a common heritage. During the same engagement, key pastoral themes were also discussed in a synodal spirit, including the ordination of priests and deacons, support for vocations, and presentations from the Vocations Office.
Lira diocese was established in 1968 on 12 July, marking the beginning of its mission of evangelization of Catholic faith and pastoral care in Lango region. Since then, the Diocese has grown steadily in faith, pastoral structures, and development, serving as a spiritual home for the faithful under its jurisdiction.
This year, the Diocesan Day celebration has been scheduled for Saturday, 11 July 2026, instead of Sunday, 12 July, which is the exact date on which the Diocese was originally established. The adjustment has been made to allow full participation of the faithful in the celebrations while still honoring the historic foundation date of Lira Diocese.
In conclusion, established on 12th July 1968, Lira Diocese has continued to grow as a vibrant community of faith, now comprising 32 parishes and 4 sub-parishes, each playing a vital role in strengthening evangelization, unity, and service to the people of God.
End

world.title: KENYA: Bishops in Kenya Decry Rising “Goonism” and Health Threats in the Country
world.desc:
Sr. Jecinter Antoinette Okoth, FSSA
Members of the Kenya Conference of Catholic Bishops (KCCB), after their plenary meeting, issued a statement on Tuesday, June 23, after their plenary, condemning a growing culture of goonism in the country and, at the same time, raising grave concerns over the proposed establishment of an Ebola quarantine and treatment facility at Laikipia Air Base.
Addressing the issue of goonism, the members of the conference, headed by Archbishop Maurice Muhatia Makumba of Kisumu Archdiocese, paid particular attention to a recent incident at All Saints Cathedral in Kenya’s capital Nairobi, where a meeting was violently disrupted by individuals who went as far as threatening police officers present at the scene.
For the Church leaders, the incident was not an isolated act of lawlessness, but a symptom of a deepening and increasingly serious threat to Kenyan society.
Violent disruptions within the sanctuary of a place of worship, they said, “constitute a blatant desecration of sacred space and a severe violation of the constitutional rights to freedom of assembly, association, and access to information.”
The bishops in their statement stressed how alarmed they were by the confidence with which the goons carried out their actions.
Recalling that they had already raised the alarm on the resurgence of goonism in a previous statement, the Bishops expressed growing fear that such violence may be enjoying official support.
They posed a challenge to the government, asking: Can the government dispel this suspicion from the minds of citizens? Is there genuine political will to deal with the menace of goonism, or does it serve the interests of the political elite?
They further warned political actors, emphasizing that places of worship are inviolable sanctuaries, not battlegrounds to be defiled by violence.
“The increasingly visible weaponization of goons to disrupt lawful public gatherings, including in houses of worship, and the intimidation of citizens seeking to participate in democratic dialogue, pose a grave threat to the rule of law and the fundamental freedoms guaranteed by Kenya's Constitution,” they said
The Kenyan Bishops demanded that the Inspector General of Police conduct a “swift, transparent investigation to unmask, arrest, and prosecute both the individuals who carry out such attacks and the sponsors behind them.” They also urged all Kenyans, including young people, to continue conducting themselves responsibly and with respect for the dignity of human life and property.
On the matter of public health, the Bishops expressed grave concern over the proposed establishment of an Ebola quarantine and treatment facility at Laikipia Air Base, which is intended primarily for American citizens.
According to the Prelates, this is an initiative imposed on Kenyans “without adequate public consultation, parliamentary oversight, or transparent disclosure of the terms governing the arrangement.”
Once again, the Bishops asked, what is in this for Kenya? Is it in the interest of the nation to welcome the treatment of foreign Ebola patients, individuals not welcome in their own country for fear of infection, while placing Kenyan citizens at risk?
The Bishops noted that the lack of public engagement over the facility had already had consequences, with lives lost in Laikipia during protests against it, residents arrested, and widespread panic spreading across the region at the prospect of an Ebola outbreak. Given the nature of Ebola and the extraordinary measures required to contain it, the KCCB members argued that government wisdom would have demanded transparent public engagement and broad consensus-building before any such facility was established.
The Bishops called on the government to engage religious leaders, civil society, healthcare workers, and affected communities, and to explore alternative solutions that strengthen Kenya's own health infrastructure and capacity to respond to Ebola and other infectious disease threats.
They also demanded transparency in all government deals affecting the country, including those relating to the exploitation of Kenya's mineral resources, reminding leaders that the nation's natural resources belong to the Kenyan people and that accountability is not optional.
The KCCB further noted that the unresolved challenges surrounding the Social Health Authority (SHA) insurance system continue to weigh on ordinary Kenyans, and signalled that these too remain on the Bishops' pastoral agenda.
Reflecting on the words of the Prophet Jeremiah, For I know the plans I have for you, declares the Lord, plans for welfare and not for evil, to give you a future and a hope (Jeremiah 29:11), the Bishops affirmed their belief in Kenya's capacity for transformation and in the power of faith to guide the nation toward justice.
They reminded Kenyans that Catholic social teaching places human dignity as the cornerstone of all justice, and calls communities to honour that dignity in one another as the foundation of genuine peace and flourishing.
They further pledged, remaining committed to walking alongside the government and the Kenyan people in this sacred work.

world.title: AMECEA: Ubuntu: African Value that Strengthens Small Christian Communities in Eastern Africa, Says Catholic Nun
world.desc:
By Sr. Henriette Anne, FSSA
Small Christian Communities (SCCs) have become central to the life of the Church; hence, greater attention should be given to rooting it into Africa’s own cultural values, especially the African Philosophical tradition of “Ubuntu”, says Sr. Jecinter Antoinnette Okoth, the author of the third topic in the new book recently published by the Pastoral Department of the Association of Member Episcopal Conferences in Eastern Africa (AMECEA), in celebrating the Golden Jubilee of SCCs in the region,
Sr. Okoth, a member of Franciscan Sisters of St. Anna and in charge of the AMECEA Online Newsletter explained in her paper on “Ubuntu as a Cultural Foundation for Small Christian Communities in AMECEA Region” that “Ubuntu-based SCCs are not merely a cultural preference or a pastoral convenience, but a lived expression of a reflective Christian reality, a form of common life rooted in the very nature of God.”
Focusing on how Ubuntu values can inform SCCs' practices, she pointed out that participatory leadership and decision-making, economic sharing and mutual aid, hospitality and inclusion are some of the ways SCCs can make good use of Ubuntu values.
According to Sr. Okoth, the participatory leadership and decision-making approach challenges hierarchical approaches that can sometimes find their way into Church structures. “When this approach is brought into SCCs, it reframes what leadership means: the role of an animator is not to direct or control but to facilitate, creating the conditions for the community itself to discern its way forward”, she said.
Drawing on the work of Fr. Joseph Healey, whose research across the AMECEA region demonstrates that thriving SCCs are those that rotate leadership, equip members with facilitation skills, and rely on consensus when making important decisions, she noted that participatory leadership and decision-making approaches do not create confusion; rather, they “honour the ubuntu spirit while drawing out the gifts that each member carries.
On economic sharing and mutual aid, the Kenyan nun expressed that in the African communities, supporting one another was not optional and that the same spirit should define SCCs.  “SCCs that are shaped by this spirit develop similar habits: members pool savings and make sure that orphans, widows, and the elderly are not left to struggle alone,” she said.
The sister further said that Ubuntu teaches that “every stranger carries the same share of humanity as anyone else and therefore deserves to be welcomed, not turned away,” a call that is inviting and challenging SCCs to stay open to other members rather than being closed and becoming exclusive.
Drawing on Agbonkhianmeghe Orobator's theological reflection, she argues that outreach is not simply an act of charity performed for strangers. Rather, it flows from recognising that “person in need is not really an outsider at all, but a member of the same human family.”
While Ubuntu values offer a rich cultural foundation for the Church, Sr. Okoth also acknowledged that there are challenges in applying Ubuntu to SCCs, including cultural diversity within the AMECEA region, stretching across Eritrea, Ethiopia, Kenya, Malawi, South Sudan, Sudan, Tanzania, Uganda, and Zambia, home to many ethnic communities; each community has its own customs, languages, and social structures.
Although Ubuntu has become widely recognised as an African philosophy, Sr. Okoth reminded readers that its origins come from the Nguni peoples of Southern Africa. Its key values, mutual care, hospitality, and community responsibility, are important across the continent. However, how these values are expressed can vary from one community to another.
“SCCs would be making a mistake,” Okoth cautions, “if they simply labelled ubuntu “African culture” and applied it everywhere as though the continent were one uniform whole. Instead, she urged that Ubuntu should be understood as a guiding philosophy rather than a rigid cultural template.
She also warns against painting African culture as if it is unchanging. African societies are always evolving. Factors like urbanization, migration, education, and globalization are changing family life, community ties, and cultural identity, and young people often navigate two worlds. They inherit community expectations based on African traditions while also facing modern cultures that focus on personal freedom and individual success. She stressed that “SCCs need to engage with this tension rather than assume that a straightforward, unbroken cultural tradition is simply available to be tapped. The cultural ground is more complicated”.
Drawing on the work of Anne Nasimiyu-Wasike, she added that authentic Ubuntu theology should reclaim communal values that honour women's wisdom while rejecting traditions that deny their dignity. “Successful ubuntu-based SCCs intentionally promote women’s leadership, ensure gender balance in decision-making, and address harmful practices such as domestic violence. They champion Ubuntu’s core idea of equality, treating everyone with dignity while setting aside any traditions that go against that goal. This requires ongoing discernment to distinguish liberating cultural wisdom from oppressive traditions”
Addressing the modernity and individualism challenge that is also facing SCCs Sr Okoth borrowed from Jesse Mugambi that the answer to this challenge “is not to dig in and defend African tradition against all outside influence, but to enter into an honest conversation between what African culture offers and what the modern world brings”.
To develop the Ubuntu-based spirit into practice, Sr. Okoth recommended that the Church in the AMECEA region put into place an intentional formation in Ubuntu Theology covering various topics, including communal values, Biblical and doctrinal roots of the Church, and how Ubuntu shapes the everyday life of SCCs.
Other recommendations included “documentation and sharing of successful, Ubuntu-based SCC practices across the region, which would prevent SCCs from reinventing solutions and build a regional identity around a shared commitment to Ubuntu-based ecclesiology”, “structural support from diocesan and parish levels and engagement with contemporary challenges.”

world.title: ERITREA: Archbishop Tesfamariam of Asmara Marks 25 Years of Episcopal Service
world.desc:
Zerayohannes Ghebrehiwet
Archbishop Menghisteab Tesfamariam, the Metropolitan Archbishop of Asmara, Eritrea, celebrated the Silver Jubilee of his episcopal ordination on Saturday, June 27, 2026, at the Cathedral of Our Lady of Perpetual Help Kidane Mehret in a solemn Mass of Thanksgiving that brought together the entire Catholic family of the Arch Eparchy of Asmara in a joyful expression of gratitude to God.
The celebration was marked by the presence of three fellow bishops, dozens of concelebrants, religious congregations, seminarians, and the faithful from all the parishes of the Arch Eparchy, as well as family members and friends of the Archbishop
Archbishop Tesfamariam was accompanied by Bishop Thomas Osman of the Barentu Eparchy, Bishop Kidanemariam Yebiyo of the Keren Eparchy, and Bishop Fikremariam Hagos of the Segeneiti Eparchy, along with Superior Generals of Monasteries and presbyter seats and 55 co-celebrants. Students of St. George Major Seminary, religious congregations, and the faithful from across the Arch Eparchy filled the cathedral for the occasion.
In his homily, Archbishop Tesfamariam began with the verse of Psalm 133: How good and how pleasant it is when brothers dwell together as one! and described it as a rich school of prayer for all seasons of life encompassing worship and praise, thanksgiving and rejoicing, supplication and repentance in equal measure, whatever circumstances a person may face.
[caption id=attachment_24952 align=alignright width=300] Archbishop's brother presenting his speech[/caption]
Turning to his own 25-year journey, he reflected on Psalm 124, Had not the Lord been with us as the honest question that comes to mind when he surveys the road travelled, and answered it with the words of Psalm 116: How can I repay the Lord for all the great good done for me? I will raise the cup of salvation and call on the name of the Lord.
What I feel today is heartfelt gratitude, he told the congregation simply.
The Archbishop said that the journey has not been without its difficulties. He acknowledged being accompanied, strengthened, challenged, and even at times defeated by the prayers, advice, and honest counsel of those around him: bishops, priests, religious, the faithful young and old, families, and friends in Eritrea and in the diaspora abroad. In these 25 years of journey, I was not alone, he said, inviting all present to join him in magnifying the Lord together.
Archbishop Tesfamariam used the occasion to trace the history of the Ge'ez Rite Catholic Church in Eritrea, which was established in 1930 with Bishop Kidanemariam Kassa as the first indigenous bishop. He named each of his five predecessors in the seat of the Arch Eparchy of Asmara: Bishop Kidanemariam Kassa, Bishop Yakob Ghebreyesus, Bishop Asratemariam Yemiru, Bishop Abraha Franswa, and Bishop Zekarias Yohannes, before noting that he himself is the sixth successor. From the Eparchy of Asmara, three further episcopal seats have been born: the Eparchies of Barentu, Keren, and Segeneiti.
With pastoral humility, the Archbishop noted that he is already praying for the bishop who will one day succeed him. Trusting in God, we did what we can, and the rest will be completed by those who succeed me, he said, offering a prayer of eternal rest for those who have gone before him and wisdom for those who will follow.
He also expressed particular devotion to two great missionary saints who have accompanied his ministry: St. Justin De Jacobi and St. Daniel Comboni. Through their intercession and example, they have played a special role in my pastoral ministry, he said, and every morning I pray for their intercession.
[caption id=attachment_24967 align=alignright width=300] Rev. Fr. Thomas presenting short life history of the archbishop[/caption]
At the conclusion of the Mass, Rev. Fr. Thomas Negash, Secretary General of the Catholic Secretariat of the Arch Eparchy of Asmara, presented a brief account of the Archbishop's life and 25 years of episcopal ministry.
Born in Berakit Abay in December 1948, Archbishop Tesfamariam entered the Comboni Minor Seminary in Dekemhare on 10th October 1963. He completed his novitiate in Ethiopia, made his first temporary vow on June 25, 1973 — the Feast of St. Daniel Comboni — and pursued studies in Philosophy in Asmara and Theology in Uganda. He was ordained a priest on February 18, 1979, by the late Bishop Abraha François, in the Comboni Fathers' convent in Asmara.
His priestly ministry took him from his home village of Berakit Abay, where he taught Catechism to children and youth, to Uganda, where he served as a missionary in Karamoja from 1979 to 1985, to Addis Ababa, and eventually to Chicago, where he served as head of students and completed a Master's Degree in Pastoral Theology, while also providing pastoral care for Catholics in San Francisco.
Returning to Eritrea, he served as Coordinator of Pastoral Activities in the Catholic Secretariat of the Eparchy of Asmara from 1998 to 2001, playing a leading role in pastoral and administrative development and guiding the first Eparchial Synod.
On June 25, 2001, he was nominated by Pope St. John Paul II to succeed the late Bishop Zecarias Yohannes and was ordained bishop on September 16, 2001, at the Cathedral of Our Lady of Perpetual Help — the same cathedral where he celebrated his Silver Jubilee a quarter of a century later.

world.title: SOUTH SUDAN: Justice and Peace Commission Empowers Women to Lead Peacebuilding in South Sudan
world.desc:
Ginaba Lino
The Justice and Peace Commission (JPC) of the Sudan and South Sudan Catholic Bishops’ Conference (SSSCBC), in partnership with Norwegian Church Aid (NCA), has trained women leaders in peacebuilding and conflict transformation in the Catholic Diocese of Torit, Eastern Equatoria State.
Women in South Sudan are increasingly being recognized as key actors in building sustainable peace and fostering reconciliation in communities affected by conflict.
The two-day training workshop brought together women from different communities under a newly launched six-month initiative known as “Together for Peace.” The project seeks to strengthen grassroots peace-building efforts, promote social cohesion, and empower women to play a more active role in preventing and resolving conflicts at the community level.
The initiative comes at a time when South Sudan continues to face localized conflicts, inter-communal tensions, and challenges related to social cohesion despite ongoing efforts to consolidate peace. Women, who are often among the most affected by violence and displacement, remain underrepresented in decision-making forums where peace and security issues are discussed.
Speaking during the opening session of the training, Fr. John Opi Severino, National Coordinator of the Justice and Peace Commission, underscored the importance of empowering women as agents of peace and reconciliation.
He explained that the project aims to strengthen community-based peace-building and conflict transformation mechanisms while rebuilding trust among communities through inclusive dialogue, reconciliation initiatives, and collaborative activities.
Fr. Opi said the initiative is designed not only to address the immediate causes of conflict but also to tackle deeper issues such as mistrust, trauma, and divisions that have persisted in communities following years of conflict.
It’s to strengthen community base peace building and conflict transformation by enhancing the capacity of women and community actors to prevent and manage conflicts in the community, to promote social cohesion and rebuild trust among communities through inclusive dialogue, reconciliation initiative and collaborative activities such as trust, division caused by war those years, to increase participation of women, youth and other marginalize groups in peaceful processes by creating safe and inclusive platform for inclusive decision making at community level, to reduce drivers of local conflicts and violence by addressing grievances such as women, youth exclusion across ethnic groups.
According to Fr. Opi, women possess unique experiences and perspectives that make them valuable contributors to peacebuilding efforts. Their involvement in mediation, reconciliation, and community dialogue can help address long-standing grievances and foster stronger social bonds among communities.
He further noted that the project seeks to create safe and inclusive spaces where women, youth, and other marginalized groups can participate meaningfully in decision-making processes. Such participation, he said, is essential for achieving sustainable peace because it ensures that the voices of all members of society are heard and considered.
The Catholic Church has long been at the forefront of promoting peace, justice, and reconciliation in South Sudan. Through its Justice and Peace Commission, the Church continues to work with local communities, government institutions, and development partners to address the root causes of conflict and strengthen peaceful coexistence.
For Norwegian Church Aid, the training represents an important investment in community resilience and inclusive peace-building.
Mr. Mini Hiteng Odihak, Program Manager for Norwegian Church Aid in Torit, emphasized that women must be recognized as key stakeholders in peace processes rather than passive observers.
He pointed out that women often bear the greatest burden during times of conflict, experiencing displacement, loss of livelihoods, and disruptions to family and community life. Despite these challenges, they frequently play crucial roles in maintaining social cohesion and supporting recovery efforts.
As Norwegian Church Aid we believe this training is important because it trains and shape women to know that they have right to participate in decision makings mainly during round table discussions regarding peace processes. Women should only just be cooks at home but they should key people in decision makings because if you look at women in case of any conflicts they are the once suffering in the community despite of their generosity in the at family and community levels.
Mr. Hiteng stressed that meaningful peace cannot be achieved if women are excluded from discussions that shape the future of their communities. He noted that empowering women with knowledge and skills in peacebuilding enables them to contribute effectively to dialogue, mediation, and conflict prevention initiatives.
The training covered various aspects of peacebuilding, including conflict analysis, dialogue facilitation, reconciliation, community engagement, and strategies for addressing the root causes of violence. Participants were encouraged to become peace ambassadors within their communities and to use the skills acquired during the training to promote peaceful coexistence.
The workshop also highlighted the importance of strengthening partnerships among faith-based organizations, government institutions, civil society organizations, and local communities in advancing peace initiatives.
Mr. Hiteng called for greater collaboration among stakeholders in Eastern Equatoria State to ensure that women are fully involved in efforts aimed at preventing conflict and building lasting peace.
In Eastern Equatoria State, we need to work together with relevant church institutions, relevant Government institutions such as ministry of peace building, peace commissions, and civil society to building women strength in peace processes.
Participants welcomed the training, describing it as a valuable opportunity to acquire practical skills and deepen their understanding of peace-building. Many expressed hope that the knowledge gained would help them contribute more effectively to reconciliation efforts in their respective communities.
The emphasis on women’s participation aligns with global and regional commitments, including the United Nations Security Council Resolution 1325 on Women, Peace and Security, which recognizes the critical role women play in conflict prevention, peace negotiations, peace-building, and post-conflict recovery.
Across Africa, faith-based organizations continue to advocate for greater inclusion of women in peace processes, recognizing that sustainable peace requires the active participation of all sectors of society. In South Sudan, where local conflicts continue to affect communities, empowering women to take leadership roles in peace-building is increasingly viewed as an essential component of long-term stability and development.
The “Together for Peace” project is a six-month initiative jointly implemented by the Justice and Peace Commission of the Sudan and South Sudan Catholic Bishops’ Conference and Norwegian Church Aid. The project planned to be implemented in Torit Diocese, Juba Archdiocese, and Wau Diocese with the goal of strengthening community-led peace-building and conflict transformation mechanisms.
The initiative focuses on promoting reconciliation, fostering social cohesion, rebuilding trust among communities affected by conflict, and addressing the root causes of violence through dialogue and inclusive engagement. It also seeks to increase the meaningful participation of women, youth, and other marginalized groups in peace processes and decision-making structures at the community level.
Through trainings, community dialogues, reconciliation activities, and collaborative peace initiatives, the project aims to equip local communities with the tools and skills needed to prevent conflict, manage disputes peacefully, and build resilient societies. Running for six months, the project is expected to contribute significantly to ongoing efforts to promote peace, justice, and sustainable development in Eastern Equatoria State and beyond.

world.title: SOUTH SUDAN: Prelate Urges Seminarians to Embrace Humility, Transformation, and Lifelong Service Through Christ
world.desc:
Ginaba Lino
Bishop Eduardo Hiiboro Kussala has called on seminarians preparing for the priesthood to embrace a life of humility, transformation, and faithful service, reminding them that authentic ministry is rooted not in personal ability but in God's enduring love and grace.
Delivering the homily during the installation of seminarians into the Ministries of Lector and Acolyte at St. Paul's Major Seminary, Bishop Hiiboro described the occasion as a celebration of hope for the Church and a powerful reminder that God continues to raise faithful servants for His people despite the challenges facing the world.
Addressing priests, religious, seminary formators, families, and guests gathered for the liturgical celebration, the bishop expressed gratitude for the opportunity to witness the formation of future priests, saying the Church has every reason to rejoice whenever young men generously respond to God's call.
Drawing inspiration from the feast of Saint Peter, Bishop Hiiboro reflected on the Apostle's transformation from a man marked by weakness and failure into the courageous leader of the early Church.
It was not his intelligence, his strength, or his past. Peter became a saint because he allowed himself to be transformed by the love of Christ.
He reminded the seminarians that their vocation does not begin with their love for God, but with God's unconditional love for them.
Before you chose Christ, He had already chosen you, the bishop said. Your vocation is founded on God's everlasting love. Never forget that.
Acknowledging the many sacrifices seminarians make on their journey toward priesthood, Bishop Hiiboro recognized that many have endured personal hardships, including the loss of loved ones, family struggles, financial difficulties, and the demanding discipline of seminary life.
He encouraged them not to view these experiences as obstacles but as part of God's plan to prepare them for faithful ministry.
He explained that a diamond forms deep beneath the earth over billions of years under immense pressure before emerging as one of the world's most precious stones.
Pressure does not destroy a diamond, he said. Pressure reveals its beauty.
Using the analogy, he told the seminarians that every lecture, examination, correction, moment of silence, rule, and challenge encountered in the seminary is part of God's process of shaping them into faithful servants.
Every difficulty is purifying you, he said. You are not ordinary people. God is forming you into something precious for His Church.
The bishop reminded those being instituted as Lectors that they are now entrusted with a sacred responsibility as official ministers of the Word of God.
The Word of God created you, he said. When you proclaim that Word, you become its faithful custodian. Treat it with reverence because it has the power to transform lives.
He also encouraged the new Acolytes to deepen their devotion to the Eucharist, describing their ministry as a privilege that brings them closer to Christ present in the Blessed Sacrament.
Although the ministries of Lector and Acolyte are transitional stages on the journey to priesthood, Bishop Hiiboro stressed that they carry profound spiritual significance and demand maturity, commitment, and faithful witness.
Turning to the realities facing Sudan and South Sudan, the bishop challenged the seminarians to become agents of healing and hope in societies wounded by conflict, violence, corruption, displacement, and suffering.
You know where you come from, he said. You know the pain and the struggles of our people. Your calling is to become part of the solution.
He urged the future priests not to distance themselves from the people they are called to serve but to accompany communities with compassion, humility, and courage.
Bishop Hiiboro further encouraged the seminarians to cultivate fraternity within the seminary by supporting one another instead of allowing pride, competition, or selfish ambition to divide them.
This seminary is a workshop where God is shaping you, he said. Be a blessing to your colleagues. Help one another grow. Correct one another with love. Build each other up.
He emphasized that true greatness in priesthood is measured not by authority or status but by humble service.
The Church does not simply need more priests, he said. She needs holy priests—priests who are close to the people, especially the young, and who faithfully reflect the heart of Jesus Christ.
Concluding his homily, Bishop Hiiboro left the seminarians with three simple yet profound truths that he urged them never to forget throughout their lives and ministry:
God is alive. God created you. God is calling you every day.
He encouraged them to walk in the unity of faith, remain humble, preach Christ faithfully, love the world with the compassion of Jesus, and become blessings wherever they are sent.
Love Jesus Christ, he said. Remain humble. Become a blessing to everyone you meet.
The celebration concluded with the formal institution of the seminarians into the Ministries of Lector and Acolyte, marking another important milestone in their preparation for the priesthood and reaffirming the Church's commitment to forming servant leaders rooted in faith, humility, and the transforming love of Christ.

world.title: MALAWI: Police Service Bids Farewell to Rev. Fr. Likhutcha After Nine Years of Dedicated Chaplaincy
world.desc:
Sam kalimba
The Malawi Police Service, in collaboration with St. Ignatius Catholic Parish, on Saturday, June 27, accorded a heartfelt farewell to Rev. Fr. Steven Alfred Likhutcha, ending his remarkable nine-year ministry as Chaplain of the Malawi Police Service.
The farewell ceremony, held at the Inspector General's Ground at Police Headquarters in Lilongwe, was attended by the Inspector General of Police, Mr. Richard Chakupaleza Chikoko Luhanga, Deputy Inspector General Dr. Noel Mlowoka Kayira, former Inspector General Rodney Jose (2018–2019), members of the Police Management Team, clergy, religious and parishioners.
The event began with a solemn Eucharistic Celebration presided over by Fr. Likhutcha and concelebrated by the Malawi Defence Force Chaplain, Rev. Fr. Augustine Likado, DP, the newly appointed Malawi Police Service Catholic Chaplain Rev. Fr. Steven Namame of the Catholic Diocese of Mangochi, Rev. Fr. John Mangwere, SAC, together with other priests.
Fr. Likhutcha's journey with the Malawi Police Service began nine years ago when he was appointed Police Chaplain by the then Episcopal Conference of Malawi, now the Malawi Conference of Catholic Bishops (MCCB). A diocesan priest of the Catholic Archdiocese of Zomba, he relocated from his home diocese to Police Headquarters in Lilongwe, which canonically falls under the Catholic Archdiocese of Lilongwe. In addition to his national chaplaincy responsibilities, he automatically became Parish Priest of St. Ignatius Catholic Parish, serving the spiritual needs of police officers, their families, and the surrounding Catholic community.
His ministry extended beyond pastoral care. As a commissioned police officer, Fr. Likhutcha faithfully served within the Malawi Police Service, rising through the ranks to become an Assistant Commissioner of Police. His unique dual vocation as both priest and police officer enabled him to provide spiritual leadership while contributing to the welfare, discipline and moral formation of police personnel.
In 2025, the Malawi Conference of Catholic Bishops appointed Rev. Fr. Steven Namame as the new Police Chaplain, marking the conclusion of Fr. Likhutcha's official assignment after nearly a decade of dedicated service.
Speaking during the farewell ceremony, Inspector General Mr. Luhanga expressed profound appreciation for Fr. Likhutcha's unwavering commitment to the Malawi Police Service. He particularly commended his efforts in strengthening the family apostolate within the police service and his pastoral leadership at St. Ignatius Parish.
The Inspector General further appealed to the Department of Spirituals, Integrity and Counselling Services to continue engaging former chaplains, saying their experience and wisdom remain valuable to the institution even after their formal assignments end.
He also acknowledged the contribution of another Catholic priest and former Police Chaplain, Rev. Fr. David Bello of the Catholic Archdiocese of Blantyre, emphasizing that the Church possesses a wealth of pastoral experience that should continue to benefit the Malawi Police Service.
In his farewell remarks, Fr. Likhutcha challenged the Department of Spirituals, Integrity and Counselling Services to remain the moral conscience of the Malawi Police Service.
The department should serve as the conscience of the institution by providing moral and spiritual guidance to all officers, including top management, so that the police serve Malawians according to the will of God, he said.
Beyond his responsibilities within the Police Service, Fr. Likhutcha actively served the wider Catholic community in the Archdiocese of Lilongwe. He was Chaplain of the Divine Mercy Apostolate, Chaplain to the Maula Deanery Choirs and an active member of the Association of Catholic Journalists, demonstrating his commitment to evangelization through various ministries.
Following the completion of his national assignment, Fr. Likhutcha has returned to his parent Diocese of Zomba, where he now serves as Parish Priest while also overseeing development initiatives as the Diocesan Projects and Investments Coordinator.
His farewell was more than a ceremonial send-off; it was a celebration of faithful service, compassionate leadership and a ministry that bridged the Church and one of Malawi's key public institutions. As he embarks on a new chapter of priestly ministry, Fr. Likhutcha leaves behind a lasting legacy of spiritual accompaniment, integrity and dedication that will continue to inspire both the Malawi Police Service and the Catholic faithful.
